message.reasoning_content:
We need to produce the completed JSON for literature_hvs_candidates.json for arxiv_id 2011.10206. We have the skeleton, paper info, inputs, and we need to fill extraction, method_chain, candidates, candidate_groups_considered.

First, analyze the paper: 591 high velocity star candidates selected from LAMOST DR7 and Gaia DR2. They define HiVelSCs with total velocities in Galactic rest-frame > 445 km/s. They use seven potential models to assess unbound probability. They say: "at least 43 HiVelSCs are unbound to the Galaxy with escape probabilities larger than 50%" (abstract) and further discussion: "at least 43 HiVelSCs have P_ub > 50% of being unbound in Watkins+2019 potential model". However, later they talk about bound/unbound status mainly in terms of probability, and they divide the sample into HVS, HRS, RS, OUT based on origin. The paper presents candidates and discusses which might be unbound.

Our goal: extract every object the paper treats as possibly unbound from the MW. That is, stars that are considered possibly unbound (escaping, unbound, HVS/HRS candidates that might escape, etc.). The paper says: "we report 591 high velocity star candidates ... with three-dimensional velocities in the Galactic rest-frame larger than 445 km s^{-1}. We show that at least 43 HiVelSCs are unbound ..." So all 591 candidates are high-velocity star candidates, but not all are necessarily unbound. The paper includes all 591 as "HiVelSCs", and then assesses their bound/unbound status. The question: which objects does the paper treat as possibly unbound? The paper says: "Among 591 HiVelSCs ... 43 HiVelSCs can escape from the Galaxy with unbound probabilities P_ub > 50% in the potential model of Watkins+2019 ... 304 HiVelSCs are unbound in the potential model of Xue+2008 ... and other 287 HiVelSCs are bound in Xue+2008 potential model with P_ub <= 50%." So the authors consider that "unbound" is model-dependent, and they present unbound probabilities for each model. The abstract says "at least 43 HiVelSCs are unbound" and "this number decreases to eight if the possible parallax zero-point error is corrected." So the paper does not claim that all 591 are unbound, but some subset. We need to include those that the paper says are possibly unbound. The paper table tab:unbound gives P_ub for the 20 fastest; many have P_ub > 50% in multiple models. The paper states that 43 have P_ub>50% in Watkins+2019, 304 in Xue+2008. So the paper identifies that at least 43 are possibly unbound (with high confidence) and up to 304 possibly unbound depending on the potential model. The paper itself likely treats those with P_ub > 50% in any of the models as "possibly unbound". The abstract says "at least 43 HiVelSCs are unbound", but also later "In our 591 HiVelSCs ... 43 ... 304 ..." So they report the numbers. The method: they select HiVelSCs as those with V_GC > 445 km/s and then estimate escape velocities. So for the purpose of inclusion, we can include those that the paper explicitly lists as unbound candidates (having P_ub > 50% in at least one model). The paper doesn't list all 43 by name; they only explicitly list the 20 fastest with all probabilities and escape velocities. The full catalog external CSV would contain all 591 with P_ub values. However, the extraction skill says: "The candidates list must be exhaustive: include every object the paper treats as possibly unbound from the Milky Way". The paper does not treat all 591 as possibly unbound. It only says that some are possibly unbound depending on the model. The paper's own statements: "at least 43 HiVelSCs are unbound" and "this number decreases to eight if the possible parallax zero-point error is corrected." That suggests that 43 are unbound under the most conservative model. Among the 20 fastest shown in tables, all have very high P_ub in at least some models. But do we include all 20? The 20 fastest are just the top 20 by V_GC. Many of them have P_ub > 0.5 in Watkins+2019 (see table-007). For instance, Hivel1: P_ub(W)=0.996, Hivel2: 0.995, etc. So indeed the 20 fastest are likely unbound with high probability. But the paper says there are 43 total with P_ub(W)>0.5. It doesn't give the IDs of all 43. So we only have explicit data for 20 (the ones in the tables). But the extraction skill requires exhaustive list of all candidates the paper treats as possibly unbound. That means we should include all objects for which the paper provides a P_ub > 0.5 in the most conservative model, or those that they consider "unbound" in their discussion. However, the paper's primary data is the external CSV, which is not available for extraction. We can't extract all 591 rows. But we can note that the paper's tables covering the 20 fastest are given. For extraction, we can only include those objects for which we have data from the provided files (the paper text, tables). The paper mentions that the full catalog is external; we don't have it. So we can only include the 20 explicitly listed candidates in the tables (those are the fastest). Are these 20 treated as possibly unbound? Yes, the paper includes escape velocities and unbound probabilities for them, showing many have high probabilities. The paper says "at least 43 HiVelSCs are unbound", and the 20 fastest are presumably among the highest velocities, so likely unbound. So we can include the 20 as possibly unbound. However, we should also check if the paper treats any other objects as possibly unbound via explicit mention. It says that 12 of their 591 were already reported in Du2, etc., but no specific names. So it's safe to include the 20 fastest as candidates. Also, the paper discusses "92 conservative HiVelSCs" and says among those, none are unbound in Watkins+2019, but 26 are unbound in Xue+2008. So those 26 are possible unbound candidates in that model. But we don't have their IDs either. So we can't list them.

Given the limitation that only the 20 fastest are tabulated with P_ub values, we can list those as candidates. Each of these is a HiVelSC. We need to extract for each: identifiers, inclusion assessment, candidate origin (introduced by this paper, since they found them), core observed phase space, derived kinematics, bound assessment, etc., using ECSV tables and text.

So we'll fill extraction.status as "candidates_found", summary describing that we extracted 20 fastest candidates, etc. But note: the total number of possibly unbound objects is 43 (or 304 depending on model), but we only have data for 20. So we might note in summary that only the 20 fastest are tabulated and extracted, the remaining candidates are in the external catalog. But the instruction says "exhaustive: include every object the paper treats as possibly unbound from the Milky Way, even when there are dozens; never truncate, sample, or pick representatives". This is a problem: we have only 20 objects with data in the provided files, but there are more possibly unbound candidates (43 under Watkins+2019). However, the paper does not list them individually; they are only present in the external CSV. We cannot extract data for them. The skill says we cannot download or parse external resources; we only use provided files. Therefore, we must limit to those for which we have direct text/tables. The candidate_groups_considered can note that the external catalog contains the full 591 candidates but we couldn't extract those not in tables. Also, we could note that the 20 fastest are included. But does the paper "treat" only these 20 as possibly unbound? No, it treats 43 as unbound in Watkins+2019, but only shows 20 in the tables. The paper's figures and text refer to the 20 fastest as a subset. So we are missing a lot. However, the skill says: "The candidates list must be exhaustive: include every object the paper treats as possibly unbound from the Milky Way, even when there are dozens; never truncate, sample, or pick representatives". That seems to require we include all unbound candidates. But we physically cannot because we lack the data. In such a case, we must set extraction.status to "needs_review" or "partial", and explain in summary that the full catalog is external and not available, and that we extracted only the 20 fastest tabulated candidates. The schema allows status "partial". Also, we can include candidate_groups_considered mentioning the external CSV as a group of candidates that were not extractable. And we'll list the 20 we have.

Thus we'll fill extraction.status: "partial", summary: "Extracted 20 fastest HiVelSCs with tabulated unbound probabilities; the full catalog of 591 HiVelSCs, including 43 with P_ub>50% in Watkins+2019, is available only as external CSV and could not be extracted." etc.

Now method_chain: we need to construct paper-level atomic method steps following the order in the paper. The paper's method: they cross-match LAMOST DR7 with Gaia DR2, select "low-f" samples (positive parallax, f_w <= 0.2), estimate distances and velocities using Monte Carlo sampling, compute escape velocities under seven potential models, select candidates with V_GC > 450 km/s or escaping in at least one model, then visual inspection of spectra to get 591 reliable. They also correct RV zero-point. Then they derive Galactocentric positions/velocities using solar position and motion (R0=8.2 kpc, z0=25 pc, v_circ=238 km/s, solar peculiar motion U,V,W = [14.0,12.24,7.25] km/s). They adopt seven potential models for escape velocity, compute P_ub as fraction of MC realizations where V_GC > v_esc. For orbit integration, they use Galpy with MWPotential2014, trace back to 10 Gyr, compute eccentricity, Zmax, rmin, energy. For origin classification they use P_gc (rmin<1 kpc), P_MW (rmin<25 kpc), P_ub thresholds. Steps: input_catalog (LAMOST DR7, Gaia DR2), cross_match (TOPCAT), quality_filter (low-f samples: positive parallax, fw <= 0.2), distance_estimation (Monte Carlo with inverted parallax), radial_velocity_measurement (LASP RV, zero-point correction), solar_position_and_motion (parameters above), velocity_calculation (Galactocentric positions and velocities), galactic_potential_model (seven potentials), escape_or_bound_assessment (compute v_esc, P_ub), sample_selection (select HiVelSCs: V_GC > 450 or escaping in any model), quality_filter (visual inspection of spectra to final 591), orbit_integration (Galpy MWPotential2014, trace back, compute e, Zmax, rmin, E-Phi), origin_assessment (classification into HVS/HRS/RS/OUT). We'll also include a step for astrometric flag quality. So method_chain: step-01 input_catalog LAMOST DR7; step-02 input_catalog Gaia DR2; step-03 cross_match; step-04 quality_filter low-f; step-05 distance_estimation; step-06 radial_velocity_measurement LASP, zero-point correction; step-07 solar_position_and_motion; step-08 velocity_calculation; step-09 galactic_potential_model for escape velocity (seven potentials); step-10 escape_or_bound_assessment; step-11 sample_selection HiVelSCs; step-12 quality_filter visual inspection to 591; step-13 orbit_integration (Galpy MWPotential2014); step-14 origin_assessment (classification). We'll add step-15 quality_filter astrometric_flag, but that is used only for conservative subset. We'll include as needed. The method_chain must have depends_on. So we'll construct accordingly.

Now candidates: 20 objects (Hivel1 through Hivel20). For each, we need to extract identifiers, inclusion, origin, core (observed, derived_kinematics, bound_assessment), plus photometry, spectroscopy, stellar_parameters, abundances, quality_flags, orbit, astrophysical_origin, extra. We'll use ECSV tables: table-001 for basic (RA, dec, S/N, class, RV, pmra, pmdec, parallax, G, GBP, GRP, astrometric_flag). table-002 for Galactocentric positions and velocities, orbit parameters (x, y, z, r_GC, Vx, Vy, Vz, V_GC, e, Zmax, rmin, E-Phi). table-006 for escape velocities (seven potentials). table-007 for unbound probabilities and origin class. table-008 for atmospheric parameters (Teff, log g, [Fe/H] from LASP and DD-Payne, [alpha/Fe] from DD-Payne). Also table-009 is data dictionary; not needed. We have to fill quantities with raw_value, value, errors, etc. For RA, Dec: from table-001: e.g., Hivel1 R.A. = 240.3374980 deg, decl. = 41.16681800 deg. These are likely in ICRS at Gaia DR2 epoch J2015.5. The paper says "The 4th to 21st columns of Table~\ref{tab:general_catalog} are listed here for the 20 fastest HiVelSCs", and the general catalog says R.A. and decl. from Gaia DR2. So coordinate frame: reference_frame ICRS, epoch J2015.5 (reference epoch). We'll set coordinate_format: decimal_degrees.

Parallax, proper motions, etc., from Gaia DR2. The radial velocity is LASP RV corrected zero-point (but table-001 shows RV_LASP with zero-point correction). So we'll use that for observed RV.

Derived kinematics: from table-002: Galactocentric x,y,z (kpc), Vx,Vy,Vz (km/s), V_GC (km/s). Also r_GC. We'll include. In derived_kinematics, we'll also include galactocentric_radius. Also tangential velocity? Not directly given, but we could compute, but we'll not add unless paper gives. We'll skip tangential, we have total velocity.

Bound assessment: escape velocity from table-006 under each potential model. However, the bound assessment fields: escape_velocity, escape_velocity_ratio, escape_margin, bound_probability, unbound_probability, bound_status_metric. We can fill escape_velocity as a range? The paper gives escape velocities for each potential model, not a single value. According to schema, we can put multiple values if needed? The schema allows for a QuantityRecord, but only one per field. We could put the Watkins+2019 escape velocity as the most conservative, or note that values depend on model. The paper uses P_ub to assess unbound. So we can set escape_velocity as the Watkins+2019 value (most conservative) with note that it depends on model. Or we can put it in extra. I think it's appropriate to put the escape velocity from the Watkins+2019 model as the primary, since they use it to define minimum unbound number. The paper says "at least 43 HiVelSCs are unbound ... in Watkins+2019 potential model". So for each candidate, we can set escape_velocity = Vesc(W) from table-006. Also we could include escape_velocity_ratio? Not directly, but we could compute V_GC / V_esc(W). The paper doesn't provide that. So we'll avoid. Escape margin: maybe difference V_GC - V_esc, but not required. We'll leave absent. Unbound probability P_ub(W) is given, so we can set unbound_probability.value = P_ub(W) as a fraction (0.996 for Hivel1). bound_probability = 1 - P_ub(W) maybe, but paper doesn't give bound prob directly; but we can compute. The schema allows bound_probability. We'll set bound_probability = 1 - P_ub(W). However note that P_ub(W) is a probability based on MC simulations; it's if V_GC > v_esc(W). So we can fill bound_probability as 1 - that, but be careful. I'll include both. Also bound_status_metric maybe the classification of origin class? Not needed. So bound_assessment will be escape_velocity (Vesc(W)), unbound_probability, and bound_probability. Provide method_refs: escape_or_bound_assessment step-10 for escape velocity and probabilities. For escape velocity, it's derived from potential model, so method_refs: step-09 (potential model) but then used in step-10. However direct producer of escape velocity values is step-10 where they compute it for each star using potential models. Actually step-09 is the potential model definition, step-10 does the computation. So method_refs for escape_velocity would be step-10. Similarly for probabilities.

Now photometry: Gaia G, G_BP, G_RP magnitudes from table-001. We'll add as photometry records with measurement_type: magnitude, band: G, G_BP, G_RP, system: Vega? Actually Gaia magnitudes are in the Vega system, but paper doesn't specify; we can set system: "Vega" (Gaia DR2). We'll include. Spectroscopy: spectral type from LAMOST 1D pipeline (col_005 Class) and radial velocity follow-up is already in observed phase space. We can add a spectroscopy record for spectral type: string "G7" etc. including method_refs (stellar_parameter_inference? but that's for stellar params). We'll put measurement_type: spectral_type, value: class string. Method: LAMOST stellar classification, step-06 perhaps. Actually the class is from LAMOST 1D pipeline, which is part of input_catalog? The table note says "Spectral type given by the LAMOST 1D pipeline". So we can attribute to input_catalog step (LAMOST DR7). So we'll put method_refs: step-01 (input_catalog LAMOST). Also S/N from LAMOST can be quality_flag.

Stellar parameters: Teff, log g, [Fe/H] from LASP and DD-Payne. We'll include in stellar_parameters teff, log_g, metallicity with both LASP and DD-Payne values. The stellar_parameters has fields teff, log_g, metallicity, mass, radius, age, luminosity, spectral_type, other. We can put teff, log_g, metallicity; we could put LASP values and DD-Payne values as separate? But the schema only allows one teff etc. So we may pick one pipeline, or include both in other. We'll put the LASP values as primary (since they are from the main pipeline) and put DD-Payne values in other as NamedQuantityRecord. Or we could put DD-Payne's [alpha/Fe] in abundances. The abundances field can include alpha abundance. So we'll create abundance record for [alpha/Fe] from DD-Payne. The DD-Payne [Fe/H] and LASP [Fe/H] differ; we'll note.

Quality flags: astrometric_flag from table-001 (0/1). We'll add quality_flag with name "astrometric_flag", value 0 or 1, description that it marks stars satisfying conservative astrometric criteria.

Orbit: eccentricity e, Zmax, rmin, E-Phi, from table-002. These are derived from orbit integration (step-13). We'll populate those fields. Also note that some have missing values (dash). The table-002 has some entries with "-" for e and Zmax, and rmin sometimes missing. For missing, we can leave field absent or set null. We'll set as null if missing. The schema allows null.

Astrophysical_origin: from table-007: origin class (col_011) like HRS, OUT, and probabilities P_gc, P_MW. We can fill origin_site as classification string? The schema's astrophysical_origin has origin_site (QuantityRecord), origin_classification, ejection_velocity, travel_time, hypothesis_metrics. We'll set origin_classification as the origin class string, and hypothesis_metrics for P_gc and P_MW. P_ub also origin probability but we already used. So we'll add hypothesis_metrics: one for P_gc (probability of origin GC), one for P_MW (probability of origin disk). These are metric_type: probability. We'll also indicate classification.

Candidate origin: These 20 stars are newly discovered as HiVelSCs in this paper, so introduced_by_this_paper. Paper only cross-matches with Du2 and OFSC, but these 20 are among the 591, and most are new. The paper says "only 14 of them were reported in the current version of the OFSC" and "12 out of our 591 HiVelSCs were already reported in Du2." So some may have been mentioned before, but not as unbound. But we treat as introduced_by_this_paper? The inclusion basis is that they are HiVelSCs with high unbound probability, introduced here. We can check if any are in OFSC or Du2; tables don't specify which ones. So for safety, we can set candidate_origin.origin_type: introduced_by_this_paper, unless the paper says it's known. I think it's safe to treat them as introduced by this paper since they are new candidates from this work. We'll note paper_reassesses_unbound_status false (they are first time assessment). For those that were previously known but not as unbound, we might need to check. The paper says "only 14 of them were reported in the current version of the OFSC", which means 14 of the total 591 were already known as high-velocity stars. Among the 20 fastest, we cannot tell which ones were in OFSC. But we can note that if any are from OFSC, they could be cited_from_literature. However, since we don't know IDs, and the paper does not provide the mapping, we can assume all 20 fastest are new (the probability is high). In inclusion summary, we can mention that some may have prior reports but not detailed. To be safe, we'll set origin_type as introduced_by_this_paper, reasoning that the paper introduces them as HiVelSCs. If later we find they were known, we adjust.

Inclusion assessment: For each candidate, the paper treats them as high-velocity star candidates, and in the tables gives unbound probabilities. So paper_labels: hvs_candidate (since they are HiVelSCs, but could be HVS/HRS/OUT). The paper classifies them as HVS candidate, HRS candidate, etc. But the overall sample is "high velocity star candidates". We can use hvs_candidate, and also the origin class labels. The schema's paper_labels includes hvs_candidate, hyper_runaway_candidate, runaway_candidate, escaping_star, unbound_star, high_velocity_star, candidate_group_member, other. Since the paper defines HiVelSCs and says some are HRS, HVS, etc., we can include multiple labels: hvs_candidate for all, and for those classified as HRS (hyper-runaway star), also hyper_runaway_candidate. For HRS candidates, the paper says they are hyper-runaway stars. So we'll assign paper_labels accordingly based on Origin_Class from table-007. For HRS candidates: ["hvs_candidate", "hyper_runaway_candidate"]; for OUT candidates: ["hvs_candidate", "high_velocity_star"]? OUT are fast halo stars, not necessarily runaway, but they are high-velocity star candidates. So we can use hvs_candidate and "high_velocity_star". The paper_labels enums include high_velocity_star, which fits. For OUT, we'll use hvs_candidate, high_velocity_star. For the ones with classification OUT, they are not necessarily unbound, but the paper says they are possibly from dwarf galaxies. The galactic_bound_claim: for stars with P_ub(W) >= 0.5, claim unbound? The paper's claim is that these HiVelSCs are "possibly unbound" depending on model. We'll set galactic_bound_claim: "possibly_unbound" for all 20 because they have high P_ub values; however, for OUT ones with lower P_ub, still possibly unbound because in some models they exceed 0.5. So we'll set possibly_unbound. The paper says "at least 43 HiVelSCs are unbound" and the 20 are among those, so they are possibly unbound. So galactic_bound_claim: possibly_unbound. inclusion_basis: explicit_unbound_text? The paper explicitly states for these stars that they have unbound probabilities > 50% in the tables and uses "unbound" in text. We'll use explicit_unbound_text. extraction_confidence: high, since data are clearly tabulated. We'll provide confidence_reason: "Tabulated unbound probabilities and velocities clearly identify them as unbound in at least one potential model." We'll source_refs from text and tables.

Now we fill each candidate. We'll use record_id: 2011.10206:cand-001 to 020. paper_candidate_id: the Hivel1 to Hivel20. gaia_source_id: from table-009 general catalog, column 3 is Gaia_designation, but not included in table-001. The table-001 only shows ID, not Gaia DR2 id. The external CSV would have it. So we don't have gaia_source_id; set empty. all identifiers: we'll list the Hivel ID and also maybe LAMOST specid? Not provided. So just Hivel ID. That's fine.

Core observed_phase_space: ra, dec, distance (heliocentric? not given in table-001 directly, but distance can be derived from parallax inverted? The paper says they use 1/parallax. In table-002 they give Galactocentric positions, but not heliocentric. Could compute distance from parallax: d = 1/parallax. For Hivel1 parallax=0.118 +/- 0.016 mas, distance = 1/0.118 = 8.47 kpc. We'll compute and include as distance. We'll put it as derived from parallax, method: distance_estimation step-05. Actually they used Monte Carlo with parallax to compute r_GC, not simple inversion. But we have inverted distance from parallax. We'll include distance quantity using raw_value of parallax from table-001, compute value. We'll set distance.value = 1/(parallax.value/1000) in kpc, error from propagation? We'll just put value from simple inversion, but note that the paper's actual distance estimation used MC and includes correlations. We'll put as derived from parallax, but as an approximation. Better to leave distance absent, because the paper's derived r_GC is Galactocentric, not heliocentric distance. However, the general catalog includes heliocentric distance r (kpc) as column 23. But not in tables. So we might skip heliocentric distance. We'll not include distance. We'll include only parallax, proper motions, radial velocity. That's okay.

Derived kinematics: Galactocentric x,y,z, Vx,Vy,Vz, V_GC, r_GC from table-002. So we'll populate those.

Bound assessment: escape_velocity: Vesc(W) from table-006 (km/s). unbound_probability: Pub(W), bound_probability: 1 - Pub(W). We'll keep raw_value as is.

Now we need to convert raw values to cleaned values. For table-001: values like "0.118 +/- 0.016" so raw_value entire string. value: 0.118, error: 0.016. For table-002: values like "-5.9 _-0.3^+0.4" which means lower_error 0.3, upper_error 0.4. We'll parse: value: -5.9, lower_error: 0.3, upper_error: 0.4. So we'll set value = -5.9, lower_error=0.3, upper_error=0.4. For escape velocities: integer values like 598, no errors. So value=598. For unbound probability: raw_value like 0.996, value=0.996, unit empty. bound_prob = 1 - 0.996 = 0.004.

Orbit parameters: e and Zmax are missing for some; we'll skip those fields (set null). For present ones: e raw like " - " for missing, so we skip. rmin values from table-002, some missing. So we'll only set if not dash. For Hivel8, rmin missing ( "- "). So we'll skip.

Now method_refs for each quantity: For observed phase space: ra, dec, parallax, proper motions from Gaia DR2 (input_catalog step-02). radial velocity from LAMOST after calibration (step-06, radial_velocity_measurement). So method_refs for those accordingly. For derived kinematics, velocity_calculation step-08. For escape velocity and probabilities, escape_or_bound_assessment step-10. For orbit, orbit_integration step-13. For stellar params from LASP: stellar_parameter_inference (step? we need to add a step for stellar_parameter_inference? The paper uses LASP pipeline, which is part of LAMOST data; we can attribute to input_catalog LAMOST or a separate step. We'll include a step for stellar_parameter_inference: step-16 maybe. But our method_chain can include: after radial_velocity_measurement, we have stellar_parameter_inference step for LASP and DD-Payne. We'll add steps for that. The paper uses DD-Payne for additional parameters, but that is also derived. So we'll add step-17 stellar_parameter_inference (DD-Payne). We'll put LASP parameters from input_catalog step. Actually LASP gives Teff, logg, [Fe/H] which are part of LAMOST catalog, so they can be from input_catalog step-01 (LAMOST DR7). So we'll set method_refs for stellar parameters from LASP to step-01. For DD-Payne, to a separate step.

We also need to include abundances from DD-Payne, method_refs to step-17.

Now let's construct method_chain steps:

Step-01: input_catalog (LAMOST DR7) - description: LAMOST DR7 low-resolution spectra, RVs and atmospheric parameters from LASP.
Step-02: input_catalog (Gaia DR2) - astrometry and photometry.
Step-03: cross_match - TOPCAT with 5 arcsec radius, produce LAMOST-Gaia catalog.
Step-04: quality_filter - select stars with positive parallax and f_varpi <= 0.2 (low-f samples), output over 8.48 million entries.
Step-05: distance_estimation - Monte Carlo distance from inverted parallax (using covariance matrix), computing Galactocentric positions and velocities. Actually the distance estimation is embedded in the velocity calculation. But we'll separate: step-05 distance_estimation (heliocentric distance), step-06 radial_velocity_measurement (LASP RV, zero-point correction), step-07 solar_position_and_motion (R0=8.2 kpc, z0=25 pc, v_circ=238 km/s, U=14.0, V=12.24, W=7.25), step-08 velocity_calculation (compute Galactocentric x,y,z,Vx,Vy,Vz,V_GC). However, the paper first selects high velocity candidates using V_GC > 450 or escaping, then later refines with LASP RV and zero-point correction. The sequence: they initially use 1D pipeline RV for selection, then after getting 1761 stars they redo with LASP RV. So actual flow: step-03 cross match, step-04 low-f, step-05 compute r_GC, V_GC using initial RVs (1D pipeline), step-06 select candidates escaping or V_GC>450 (sample_selection), step-07 visual inspection to 591 (quality_filter), step-08 recompute with LASP RV and zero-point correction (radial_velocity_measurement and velocity_calculation redo). To simplify, we'll make method_chain as logical: step-03 cross match, step-04 low-f, step-05 distance estimation using simple inverse parallax? Actually they do MC with covariance, but that's part of velocity calculation. We'll combine distance and velocity calculation in one step? Skill says do not combine distance, velocity, orbit, escape/bound in one broad step. So we'll have: step-05 distance_estimation (heliocentric distance from parallax, using inversion and later MC). But they use MC to account for uncertainties, but that is part of velocity calculation. We'll have step-05 distance_estimation (parallax inversion with MC samples). Then step-06 radial_velocity_measurement (initial 1D pipeline RV, then later LASP recalibration). We'll make two sub-steps: initial RV from LAMOST 1D pipeline (step-06a), then later recalibration with LASP (step-06b). But method_chain should be linear. We'll have step-05 distance_estimation, step-06 radial_velocity_measurement (initial RVs), step-07 solar_position_and_motion, step-08 velocity_calculation (compute Galactocentric positions and velocities from 5D astrometry and RV). Then step-09 sample_selection (select from over 8.48 million those with V_GC>450 or escaping under any potential model, using initial RVs; get 31,440). Step-10 quality_filter (spectral quality, keep highest S/N, 1761 left). Step-11 radial_velocity_measurement (LASP RV with zero-point correction, recalculation of V_GC for 1761 -> 591 after visual check). Step-12 velocity_calculation (recompute V_GC using LASP RVs). Step-13 escape_or_bound_assessment (compute escape velocities under seven potentials, P_ub). Step-14 orbit_integration (Galpy MWPotential2014, trace back). Step-15 origin_assessment (compute P_gc, P_MW, classification).
